title: wave-reviewer: factory-check-Kriterien absorbieren
description:
## Warum

wave-reviewer prüft aktuell nur cross-bead Strukturprobleme (dependency ordering, ownership collisions, lifecycle contradictions). factory-check prüft pro Bead die Spec-Qualität (AC-Vollständigkeit, Scenario-Präsenz, Design-Notes). Im Kontext einer Wave-Review-Gate (siehe Folge-Bead) will der wave-orchestrator EINE konsolidierte Review-Ausgabe — Struktur + Bead-Qualität zusammen — damit er ohne zusätzliche Round-Trips editieren kann.

## Scope

- wave-reviewer references/checklist.md um factory-check-Kriterien erweitern (AC-Vollständigkeit, Scenario für features, Validation-Sections, Design-Notes)
- Output-Format erweitern: pro Bead zusätzlich einzeiliger Quality-Score (A/B/C)
- Bei Quality < B automatisch MEDIUM-Finding generieren
- /factory-check bleibt als Standalone-Skill erhalten (für Single-Bead-Nutzung außerhalb Wave-Kontext)

## Acceptance Criteria

- wave-reviewer Output enthält zusätzliche Spalte 'Bead Quality' (A/B/C) pro Bead
- Bei Quality < B wird automatisch eine MEDIUM-Finding mit Kategorie 'bead_quality' in der Findings-Tabelle erzeugt
- factory-check-Kriterien dokumentiert in references/checklist.md (Sektion 'Per-Bead Quality Checks')
- /factory-check Skill funktioniert standalone unverändert (keine Breaking Changes)

## Out of Scope

- Integration in wave-orchestrator (das ist Folge-Bead)
- Codex-Routing (das ist Teil des Folge-Beads)
notes: Wave 1 report: A/B/C rubric contract landed; 3 review regressions caught before shipping. Unblocks Phase 1.25.
close_reason: Absorbed factory-check quality criteria into wave-reviewer: A/B/C per-bead scoring, auto MEDIUM finding for grade C, checklist documented in references/checklist.md; 2 review iterations, 3 regressions fixed (B-trigger, B/C rubric, output ordering)

title: [feature] TaskCreated hook — per-subagent metrics + conditional standards injection
description:
## Problem

Two related problems share the same trigger (Agent/Task spawn):

1. **Standards injection is manual**: CLAUDE.md instructs 'Vor Subagent-Start /inject-standards --mode=paths aufrufen'. This is forgotten regularly. No enforcement mechanism.
2. **No per-subagent cost metrics**: `bead-metrics` tracks per-bead cost, but not 'which agent type is most expensive per bead?' or 'is reviewer overhead justified?'.

## Proposed Solution

A TaskCreated hook (or equivalent Agent-spawn mechanism) that:

1. **Injects standards conditionally based on `subagent_type`** — not universally
2. **Logs per-subagent token cost** (continuing through TaskOutput/TaskStop)

## Standards Injection — Routing by subagent_type

Mapping table at `~/.claude/agent-standards.yml`:

```yaml
dev-tools/*:  [english-only, no-emoji, tool-standards]
medical/*:    [english-only, compliance, content-isolation]
core/*:       [english-only]
beads-workflow/*: [english-only]
# Read-only agents — no standards needed
Explore:      []
researcher:   []
general-purpose: []
```

Rules:
- Only inject for agents with Write/Edit/Bash in toolset (structural check via agent definition).
- Skip for read-only agents — their output doesn't need coding standards, and injection bloats their context.
- Injection format: prepend standards *paths* to agent prompt (not full content), let agent read them lazily.

## Metrics

- Extend existing `~/.claude/metrics.db` schema with per-agent rows
- Capture: subagent_type, parent_bead_id (if any), tokens_in, tokens_out, duration, model
- Feed into `bead-metrics` skill for 'impl:review:verify ratio per subagent type' analysis

## Open Research Questions (resolve before impl)

- Does Claude Code expose a TaskCreated/AgentSpawn hook today, or only TaskOutput/TaskStop? Research first.
- If no pre-spawn hook exists, is injection possible via system-prompt wrapper or via modifying the Agent() call itself? Test compatibility.

## Scope

**In scope:**
- TaskCreated hook (or equivalent) in `~/.claude/hooks/`
- `agent-standards.yml` mapping + `standards/dev-tools/subagent-standards.md` doc
- Metrics schema extension + bead-metrics skill update

**Out of scope:**
- Dynamic standards composition beyond the mapping table
- Cost-based routing decisions (alerting only, not automatic agent switching)
acceptance_criteria: 1) Research question resolved: document whether TaskCreated hook exists and chosen injection mechanism. 2) On Agent spawn, standards injected ONLY for agents matching agent-standards.yml pattern AND having Write/Edit/Bash tools. 3) Read-only agents (Explore, researcher, general-purpose) receive NO injection. 4) Per-subagent cost logged to metrics.db with subagent_type, tokens, duration, model. 5) bead-metrics skill shows per-subagent breakdown. 6) agent-standards.yml documented in standards/dev-tools/subagent-standards.md. 7) Off-switch via env var. 8) entropy-scan passes. 9) Spawn overhead <100ms.
notes:
[WAVE-REVIEW 2026-04-21]

Severity: MEDIUM (1), LOW (1). Source: /wave-reviewer + Hook-Research 2026-04-21.

### Research-Outcome: TaskCreated-Hook existiert (mit Nuance)
Dokumentiert in Claude Code v2.1.89 (2026-03-31). Fires VOR Subagent-Spawn
via Task-Tool. Kann mit {"decision": "block"} Spawn verhindern.

ABER: SubagentStart-Hook existiert NICHT (Issue #14859 offen seit Dez 2025).
Konsequenz fuer Metrics: TaskCreated liefert Spawn-Zeitpunkt, SubagentStop
liefert Completion. Duration/Tokens muessen aus Differenz der beiden
Ereignisse ODER aus SubagentStop-Payload allein gewonnen werden.

Quellen: claudelog.com v2.1.89; blakecrosley.com (26 lifecycle events);
GitHub issue #14859 fuer SubagentStart-Absenz.

### MEDIUM-1: Metrics-Claim kollidiert mit existierendem agent_calls-Table
~/.claude/metrics.db hat bereits eine agent_calls-Tabelle mit den gesuchten
Feldern:
  run_id, bead_id, wave_id, phase_label, agent_label, model, iteration,
  input_tokens, cached_input_tokens, output_tokens, reasoning_output_tokens,
  total_tokens, duration_ms, exit_code, recorded_at

Sie wird heute vom bead-orchestrator via insert_agent_call() im Subagent-Prompt
gefuellt ("Metrics Logging"-Section bead-orchestrator.md:460-469).

Original-AC "Extend existing metrics.db schema with per-agent rows" ist daher
entweder (a) Duplikat oder (b) Schema-Drift.

Concrete AC-Update:
- Hook schreibt in die EXISTIERENDE agent_calls-Tabelle (nicht neue
  Parallel-Tabelle), KEY: run_id + bead_id + agent_label + iteration.
- Wenn Orchestrator und Hook denselben Call loggen: Hook schreibt ersten
  Stub-Row bei TaskCreated (tokens=0, duration=0), Orchestrator updated
  oder rep overwriten bei Completion. ODER Hook loggt nur, wenn Orchestrator
  es NICHT tut (Ad-Hoc-Spawn ohne bead-orchestrator-Context).
- Entscheidung + Mechanismus in AC festhalten.

### LOW-1: agent-standards.yml-Routing-Tabelle braucht Trinity-Framing
Routing-Tabelle ("dev-tools/*: [english-only, ...]") ist de facto ein
Enforcer-Proactive: sie formt den Agent-Prompt VOR Spawn so, dass falsche
Patterns schwerer werden. Trinity-Vokabular explizit in
standards/dev-tools/subagent-standards.md benennen hilft der zukuenftigen
Wartung.

### KEIN BLOCKER MEHR: Hook-Verfuegbarkeit geklaert
AC #1 ("Research question resolved: document whether TaskCreated hook exists
and chosen injection mechanism") kann ergaenzt werden um:
"Ergebnis: TaskCreated existiert v2.1.89+. Injection-Mechanismus: Prepend
 der im Mapping gelisteten Standards-Pfade (nicht Content) an task_description
 via PreSpawn-Manipulation, falls vom Hook unterstuetzt. Fallback:
 system-prompt-Wrapper beim Agent-Tool-Invoke."
Damit ist AC #1 factual resolved; Impl-ACs #2-9 koennen parallelisiert werden.

[WAVE-REVIEW-DECISIONS 2026-04-21]

Source: /wave-reviewer CCP-rzf CCP-wl6 CCP-mit + HITL clarification.

## HIGH — run_id Propagation (RESOLVED via HITL: Conditional Hook)
Decision: Hook does NOT try to share run_id with orchestrator. Instead, hook acts only when orchestrator is NOT the caller.
Mechanism: Orchestrator exports CCP_ORCHESTRATOR_RUN_ID (or equivalent marker) into the child environment before Agent() spawn. Hook reads the env var:
- If present: skip metrics write entirely (orchestrator owns the row via insert_agent_call at its phase end).
- If absent: treat as ad-hoc Agent() spawn, write single row at SubagentStop with generated run_id="adhoc-<uuid>" and phase_label="adhoc".
Action: Add AC: "Hook detects CCP_ORCHESTRATOR_RUN_ID env var. If present, exits without write. If absent, writes exactly one row to agent_calls at SubagentStop."

## HIGH — Missing update_agent_call API (OBSOLETED BY DECISION)
The "stub at TaskCreated + update at SubagentStop" design is discarded. Only insert_agent_call() is used.
Action: Drop any AC references to stub rows / TaskCreated writes for metrics. TaskCreated remains the hook trigger ONLY for standards injection; metrics writes happen at SubagentStop.

## MEDIUM — Dual-Writer Collision (RESOLVED via Conditional Hook)
No overlap: orchestrator owns orchestrated spawns, hook owns ad-hoc spawns. Keyed by run_id disjunction.

## MEDIUM — Mixed Scope (OPEN — user decision needed)
Bead bundles two concerns behind one trigger:
(a) Conditional standards injection via agent-standards.yml (Enforcer-Proactive)
(b) Per-subagent metrics for ad-hoc spawns (logging)
Recommendation: Split into CCP-wl6a (standards injection) and CCP-wl6b (metrics), both triggered by TaskCreated/SubagentStop but owned/tested independently. Either can fail without blocking the other.
Action: User decides split vs keep-bundled before dispatch.

## MEDIUM — Bead Quality Score C (OPEN)
Gaps: no MoC table; metadata.intent MISSING; AC #1 ("Research question resolved...") is now factually obsolete (research done in notes).
Action: Drop AC #1. Add MoC table. Populate metadata.intent.

## LOW — Trinity Vocabulary (OPEN)
- agent-standards.yml = Enforcer-Proactive (shapes prompt before spawn)
- standards/dev-tools/subagent-standards.md = ADR (routing-by-subagent-type law)
- TaskCreated hook = the Enforcer-Reactive checkpoint that applies the routing
Action: Name these explicitly in Scope section of standards/dev-tools/subagent-standards.md and in the bead body.

[SUPERSEDED 2026-04-21]

Split into two beads per wave-review decision:
- CCP-dzk — Standards injection (Enforcer-Proactive via agent-standards.yml)
- CCP-2n7 — Per-subagent metrics (conditional hook for ad-hoc spawns)

Both inherit the design decisions captured in the WAVE-REVIEW-DECISIONS note above. Closing this bead in favor of the two children.
close_reason: superseded by CCP-dzk (standards injection) + CCP-2n7 (metrics) per wave-review split 2026-04-21

title: [feature] PermissionDenied hook — realtime permission-learning loop (HITL)
description:
## Problem

`fewer-permission-prompts` skill scans transcripts retrospectively to suggest allowlist entries. By the time the batch runs, the context of why something was denied is stale. A realtime feedback loop would close the gap.

**Complement, not replacement**: this bead adds a realtime signal; the existing skill continues to run retrospectively as the batch-cleanup path.

## Proposed Solution

A PermissionDenied hook (Claude Code v2.1.89+, auto-mode denials only) that logs every auto-mode deny (tool + pattern + cwd + cmdline + timestamp). After N denies of the same pattern WITHOUT an intervening approval of that pattern, generate a draft suggestion for `permissions.yml` or a new `rules.d/*.txt` entry.

## Design Constraints (non-negotiable)

- **HITL, never auto-apply**: Hook produces draft patches only. User approves/rejects. Writing permissions.yml automatically erodes the safety model.
- **Classification before suggestion**: Every deny is classified as safe-read / mutating / destructive. Destructive denies (e.g. `rm -rf`, `dolt push --force`) NEVER produce allowlist suggestions even if repeated.
- **Threshold logic**: Trigger is 'N denies without intervening approval of the same pattern'. Prevents learning wrong patterns for commands the user intentionally decides case-by-case.

## Pattern Normalization (Key Definition)

Deterministic pattern key = `tool_name + ":" + first_arg_head + ":" + cwd_project_root`

- `tool_name`: e.g. `Bash`, `Write`
- `first_arg_head`: for Bash = first token of cmdline (e.g. `git`, `rm`, `bd`); for Write = basename of target path; for generic tools = tool-specific first parameter head
- `cwd_project_root`: nearest ancestor containing `.git/` or `.beads/`, or `~` if none

Collapses: different absolute paths under same project; different targets of same command; different cmdline args after the first token.

Does NOT collapse: different projects (learning is project-scoped), different tool names, different first commands.

Full pattern-key spec lives in `standards/dev-tools/permission-classifier.md` Section 2.

## Storage

- Raw deny log: append-only `~/.claude/permission-denies.jsonl` (rotated weekly)
- Aggregated suggestion queue: `~/.claude/permission-suggestions.json`
- Surface: stdout hook message on threshold hit OR weekly digest

## Method of Change (MoC)

| Measure | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| % of auto-mode denies captured with classifier label | 0 | 100 |
| Destructive-deny suggestions generated | — | 0 (always blocked) |
| Repeated-pattern draft suggestions surfaced | 0 (batch-only via fewer-permission-prompts) | ≥ threshold-triggered count per week |
| Hook overhead per deny event | — | <50ms |

## Scope

**In scope:**
- PermissionDenied hook in `~/.claude/hooks/` (fleet-global, auto-mode only)
- Classifier rules documented in `standards/dev-tools/permission-classifier.md` (ADR for safe-read/mutating/destructive classification)
- Draft-suggestion generator Helper (NOT the apply mechanism — user manually edits permissions.yml)

**Out of scope (v1):**
- Interactive-mode denies (user clicks "Deny" in permission prompt) — tracked in follow-up bead CCP-8wm via PreToolUse-block parsing.
- Auto-apply
- Cross-session pattern detection
- UI for reviewing queue (CLI-only v1)

## Trinity Labels

- `standards/dev-tools/permission-classifier.md` = **ADR** (classification law: safe-read / mutating / destructive)
- PermissionDenied hook = **Enforcer-Reactive** (logs denies post-event, after auto-mode classifier)
- Draft-suggestion generator = **Helper** (produces draft patches for user review)
acceptance_criteria: 1) Hook fires on every auto-mode PermissionDenied event and persists structured JSON entry to ~/.claude/permission-denies.jsonl. 2) Interactive-mode denies are explicitly out-of-scope for v1 (tracked in follow-up bead CCP-8wm). 3) Classifier labels every deny as safe-read/mutating/destructive, documented in standards/dev-tools/permission-classifier.md. 4) Pattern key = tool_name+':'+first_arg_head+':'+cwd_project_root (deterministic, documented in classifier doc). 5) After N denies (default 3) of same pattern key without intervening approval, a draft suggestion is generated. 6) Destructive denies NEVER generate suggestions even if repeated. 7) Suggestion is surfaced to user (stdout on threshold hit OR weekly digest), NOT auto-applied. 8) Off-switch via env var (e.g. CCP_NO_PERMISSION_LEARNING=1) or settings flag. 9) Scenario covers happy path + destructive-block path + off-switch path + pattern-collapse fixture + cross-project isolation. 10) entropy-scan passes. 11) Hook overhead <50ms per deny event (p95 over 20 runs).
notes:
[WAVE-REVIEW 2026-04-21]

Severity: MEDIUM (1) nach Research. Source: /wave-reviewer + Hook-Research 2026-04-21.

### Research-Outcome: PermissionDenied-Hook existiert
Hinzugefuegt in Claude Code v2.1.89 (2026-03-31/04-01). Fires nach auto-mode
classifier denials. Rueckgabe {"retry": true} erlaubt Model-Retry.

Quellen: claudelog.com Changelog v2.1.89; blakecrosley.com (26 lifecycle
events als of v2.1.116); GitHub anthropics/claude-code.

### MEDIUM-1: Scope-Einschraenkung dokumentieren (nur auto-mode, nicht interactive-deny)
PermissionDenied fires NUR fuer auto-mode classifier denials. Wenn der User
im interaktiven Permission-Prompt "Deny" klickt, feuert KEIN Hook -- der
einzige pre-call Interception-Point dafuer ist PreToolUse mit
{"decision": "block"}.

Konsequenz: "log every deny" laesst sich so nicht implementieren. Entweder:
(a) Scope auf auto-mode denies einschraenken und in Acceptance/Design
    dokumentieren, ODER
(b) Zweiten Pfad ueber PreToolUse-Block-Parsing ergaenzen (aufwendiger,
    weil PreToolUse auch fuer Erlaubnisse feuert).

Empfehlung: v1 auf auto-mode beschraenken, interactive-denies als Follow-up
filen. Nicht-blocking fuer Dispatch, aber AC-Formulierung muss das scope-ern.

### LOW: fewer-permission-prompts-Skill als Konsument referenziert
Das bestehende Skill macht retrospektives Scanning. Dieser Bead komplementiert
das mit realtime-Signal. Nicht-blocking, aber in Description sollte ein
expliziter "Komplement, kein Ersatz"-Satz stehen.

### KEIN BLOCKER MEHR: Hook-Verfuegbarkeit geklaert
Die urspruenglich im Wave-Review angemahnte Research-Gate (AC #0)
entfaellt -- Hook ist verfuegbar. Implementierung kann direkt starten.

[WAVE-REVIEW-DECISIONS 2026-04-21]

Source: /wave-reviewer CCP-rzf CCP-wl6 CCP-mit + HITL clarification.

## MEDIUM — AC #1 Scope (RESOLVED via HITL)
Decision: v1 scoped to auto-mode classifier denials only. Interactive-deny path deferred to follow-up bead.
Actions before impl:
- Rephrase AC #1: "Hook fires on every auto-mode PermissionDenied event and persists structured JSON entry."
- Add OOS line in Scope: "Interactive-mode denies (user clicks Deny in permission prompt) are out of scope for v1; tracked in follow-up bead via PreToolUse-block parsing."

## MEDIUM — Pattern Normalization Undefined (OPEN)
AC #3 threshold ("N denies of same pattern without intervening approval") depends on a deterministic pattern key. Design Constraints mention collapsing semantically equivalent invocations but never specify the rule.
Action: Add AC that defines the pattern key, e.g. "Pattern key = tool_name + first_positional_arg_head (2 chars of cmdline for Bash) + cwd" OR cite a spec section in standards/dev-tools/permission-classifier.md.

## MEDIUM — Bead Quality Score C (OPEN)
Gaps: no MoC table; metadata.intent MISSING (feature-type requirement per wave-reviewer rubric).
Action: Add 2-3 row MoC (Method-of-Change) table describing how success is measured (e.g., "% of repeated denies that generate a draft suggestion"). Populate metadata.intent.

## LOW — Trinity Vocabulary (OPEN)
Name the roles explicitly in Scope section:
- standards/dev-tools/permission-classifier.md = ADR (classification law: safe-read/mutating/destructive)
- PermissionDenied hook = Enforcer-Reactive (logs denies post-event)
- Suggestion generator = Helper (produces draft patches for user review)

## FOLLOW-UP (recommendation, not auto-filed)
File a new bead "PermissionDenied v2 — cover interactive denies via PreToolUse-block parsing" AFTER CCP-rzf closes. Blocked-by: CCP-rzf. Scope: add a second path that inspects PreToolUse blocks whose decision == 'block' and attributes them as user-driven denies. Keep overhead concern in mind (PreToolUse fires for every tool call).

metadata.intent: Close the gap between deny-events and permission-learning by logging auto-mode PermissionDenied events in realtime and producing classifier-gated draft suggestions for permissions.yml that the user reviews before applying. Complements fewer-permission-prompts skill (retrospective scanner) with a realtime signal.

title: PreCompact/PostCompact memory bridge — snapshot WIP state before context compaction
description:
## Problem

Claude Code triggers automatic context compaction when approaching token limits. The compaction replaces older messages with a summary, which frequently loses **actionable WIP state**: the specific bead being worked on, the current debugging hypothesis, half-finished file edits, or the decision that was just made but not yet acted on.

CLAUDE.md + rules.d get re-injected after compaction, but session-specific context does not. Today the only recovery path is manual: re-read git status, re-read beads, re-read recent files.

## Proposed Solution

Add two hooks:

1. **PreCompact hook** — before compaction fires, snapshot the current WIP state into open-brain as a structured memory with a recoverable tag.
2. **PostCompact hook** — after compaction, inject the snapshot back into the context so Claude can resume without re-deriving state from scratch.

## Snapshot Contents

A WIP snapshot captures:

- Current working directory (absolute path)
- Active bead ID(s) if any (parsed from recent `bd show` output or recent `bd` commands)
- Last 5 files touched (from git status + recent Edit/Write tool calls)
- Last 3 tool errors if any
- "What I was about to do next" — best-effort extraction from the last assistant turn before compaction

## Storage Strategy

Use existing `mcp__open-brain__save_memory` with:
- `memory_type`: `session_wip`
- `tags`: `[session_id:<id>, project:<basename of cwd>, wip_snapshot]`
- Content: structured JSON string

Retrieval uses existing `mcp__open-brain__search` with tag filter. No new open-brain tools needed for v1 — revisit if retrieval UX is clunky.

**Lifecycle:** WIP snapshots are ephemeral by design. Add a cleanup policy (follow-up bead): memories tagged `wip_snapshot` older than 7 days auto-archive during the open-brain lifecycle pipeline.

## Hook Wiring

- PreCompact: shell script reads cwd, git status, recent tool calls from events.db, builds JSON, calls `save_memory` via MCP CLI or HTTP endpoint.
- PostCompact: shell script searches open-brain for the most recent `session_id:<id>` WIP snapshot, injects content via hook stdout as additional context.

## Scope

**In scope:**
- PreCompact + PostCompact hooks in `.claude/hooks/` (fleet-global, not per-project)
- JSON snapshot schema documented in `standards/dev-tools/memory-bridge.md`
- Smoke test: trigger manual compaction, verify snapshot roundtrip

**Out of scope:**
- Dedicated `save_wip_snapshot` / `load_wip_snapshot` MCP tools on open-brain (follow-up if needed)
- TTL/auto-archive in open-brain (follow-up bead)
- Cross-session stitching (snapshot from yesterday auto-loading today) — explicit `/resume-wip` skill instead

## Acceptance Criteria

1. PreCompact hook fires reliably before compaction (verified via events.db log entry)
2. Snapshot persists to open-brain as queryable memory with documented tags
3. PostCompact hook retrieves the most recent snapshot for the current session and injects it
4. After forced compaction in a test session, Claude can answer "which bead were we working on?" without re-reading git or bead state
5. Hook failures degrade gracefully — compaction still succeeds, error logged but not blocking
6. Schema documented in `standards/dev-tools/memory-bridge.md` with example snapshot
7. `entropy-scan` skill passes (no new invariant violations)

## Design Notes

- Hooks must be **fast** (<500ms) or compaction blocks noticeably. Prefer direct HTTP call to open-brain over MCP roundtrip if latency is an issue.
- Use session_id from Claude Code env (if exposed) or fallback to `uuidgen` stored in `/tmp/claude-session-<pid>`.
- PostCompact must not dominate context — cap snapshot injection at ~2k tokens, truncate oldest-first.
- Reference: Golden System Prompt "Context awareness" section — this implementation is the counterpart to "Save current progress and state to memory before the context window refreshes."


## Required Skills
bash,hooks,mcp,open-brain
acceptance_criteria: 1) PreCompact hook fires before compaction. 2) Snapshot persists to open-brain with session_wip type + wip_snapshot tag. 3) PostCompact retrieves + injects most recent snapshot. 4) After forced compaction, Claude knows active bead without re-reading state. 5) Hook failures degrade gracefully (non-blocking). 6) Schema documented in standards/dev-tools/memory-bridge.md. 7) entropy-scan passes.

title: [REFACTOR] bead-orchestrator aggregiert Subagent-Debriefs in phase_summaries
description:
## Context

Teil der "Single Source of Truth pro Session"-Architektur. Blocks auf CCP-9bm (Subagent Debrief Return Contract).

Heute sammelt bead-orchestrator freitext-3-Zeilen-Phase-Summaries (bead-orchestrator.md:81-95). Mit dem neuen Debrief-Contract bekommt er strukturierte Debriefs zurück, die er akkumulieren und an session-close übergeben kann.

## Goal

bead-orchestrator parsed und aggregiert Debriefs aller seiner Subagents (10-15 pro Bead-Lauf). Output: eine strukturierte Aggregation, die session-close als canonical input nutzt — keine direkten open-brain Writes mehr aus dem Orchestrator-Pfad.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] bead-orchestrator.md (in plugins/core oder wo er lebt) erweitert: nach jedem Subagent-Spawn den Debrief-Block extrahieren via fixed-heading-parser aus dem Standard
- [ ] phase_summaries-Struktur erweitert: zusätzlich zu 3-Zeilen-Summary jetzt auch Debrief-Aggregate (alle Discoveries/Decisions/Surprises/Follow-ups aus allen Subagents)
- [ ] Parser-Reference aus CCP-9bm wiederverwenden (keine Neuimplementierung)
- [ ] Handoff-Format spezifiziert: wie bead-orchestrator das Aggregat an session-close weitergibt (env-var, file, return-payload?) — Entscheidung + Dokumentation
- [ ] Subagents die KEINEN Debrief liefern (Explorer, Researcher) werden nicht geparsed, das ist by design
- [ ] Test-Szenario: ein Bead-Lauf mit Minimal-Mock-Subagents, die je einen Debrief-Block liefern, führt zu einer korrekten Aggregation

## Non-Goals

- Orchestrator selbst schreibt etwas nach open-brain (das macht weiterhin session-close)
- Änderung der existierenden 3-Zeilen-phase_summaries (die bleiben parallel für visuelle Status-Darstellung)

## Depends on

CCP-9bm (Subagent Debrief Return Contract)
notes:
[WAVE-REVIEW 2026-04-21]

Severity: HIGH (1), MEDIUM (1). Source: /wave-reviewer over Debrief-Chain.

### HIGH-1: Handoff-Mechanismus muss eingeschraenkt werden (kein return-payload)
bead-orchestrator.md:896 spawnt heute: Agent(subagent_type="core:session-close")
OHNE prompt-Argument. session-close laeuft als *downstream* Subagent des
Orchestrators -> es gibt keinen return-payload-Kanal, ueber den das Aggregat
hochfliessen koennte.

Die originale AC liess "env-var, file, return-payload?" als offene Wahl stehen.
Das ist strukturell falsch: return-payload ist unmoeglich.

Concrete AC-Update:
- Handoff-Format darf NICHT return-payload sein
- Zulaessige Mechanismen: (a) Umgebungsvariable mit Pfad zu JSON-File,
  (b) fester Pfad $REPO_ROOT/.worktree-handoff.json, (c) prompt-String
  beim session-close-Spawn. Genau EINEN Mechanismus waehlen + dokumentieren.
- session-close.md muss nach dieser Aenderung den Handoff *lesen*, nicht nur
  "erkennen" -> concrete read-path ist Teil der AC.

### MEDIUM-1: Parser wiederverwenden, nicht reimplementieren
Entscheidung in CCP-9bm (User-Freigabe 2026-04-21): parse_debrief ist
aufrufbarer Python-Helper unter beads-workflow/lib/orchestrator/parse_debrief.py,
Invocation via Bash (stdin -> JSON stdout).

Concrete AC-Update:
- bead-orchestrator ruft den Helper via Bash auf, z.B.:
  python3 beads-workflow/lib/orchestrator/parse_debrief.py < subagent-output.txt
- Keine Prompt-Level-Regex-Duplikation im Orchestrator-Spec
- Aggregation (Merge der vier Listen ueber alle Subagents) wird in einem
  zweiten Helper-Script ODER inline beschrieben

### Abgeleitete Aenderung Headings
CCP-9bm adoptiert Step-11-Taxonomie. Consumer hier nutzt identisch:
key_decisions / challenges_encountered / surprising_findings / follow_up_items.

title: [FEATURE] Subagent Debrief Return Contract
description:
## Context

Aktuell gibt es drei konkurrierende Pfade für Session-Knowledge in open-brain:
1. Stop-Hook → /api/summarize (feuert pro Turn, projekt-weiter Fetch → Dubletten-Pollution)
2. SubagentStop-Hook → dito (~15 Calls pro Bead-Orchestrator-Lauf)
3. core:session-close agent → hochwertige Summary mit Commit/Bead-Kontext

Der Hook-Pfad ist Relikt aus der Zeit vor bead-orchestrator und wird ersetzt. Dieser Bead ist der erste Schritt: Subagents produzieren strukturierte Debriefs als Teil ihres Return-Payloads, statt selbst nach open-brain zu schreiben.

## Goal

Definiere einen verbindlichen Return-Format-Contract, den ALLE Subagents (Implementer, Test-Author, Reviewer, etc.) am Ende ihrer Ausführung einhalten. Der Parent-Agent (bead-orchestrator / wave-orchestrator) sammelt diese Debriefs in phase_summaries und übergibt sie an session-close für den kanonischen Memory-Write.

## Spec: Fixed-Heading Markdown Format

Jeder Subagent hängt an seine finale Message einen Debrief-Block an:

```markdown
## Return Format (append to your final message)

### Debrief

#### Discoveries
- <things I learned about the codebase / system>

#### Decisions  
- <non-obvious choices I made and why>

#### Surprises
- <things that did not match expectations>

#### Follow-ups
- <refactor/bug notes for future beads>
```

Gründe für fixed-heading Markdown statt JSON-Fence:
- LLM-freundlich (keine Format-Brüche)
- Parent parsed deterministisch via Heading-Split (h3 "Debrief" + h4 Sections)
- Leerzulassen einzelner Sections erlaubt (z.B. keine Surprises)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Neuer Standard: malte/standards/agents/debrief-contract.md mit Format-Spec + Parse-Reference
- [ ] Template-Block im Contract, 1:1 copy-paste-fähig in Agent-Prompts
- [ ] Parser-Reference in Python (≤ 30 Zeilen): parse_debrief(text: str) -> Debrief mit 4 Listen
- [ ] Sample-Output + Parse-Test als Beispiel im Standard
- [ ] Agent-Update-Guide: welche Agents im Plugin diesen Contract lernen müssen (primär implementer, test-author, test-engineer, review-agent, verification-agent; nicht nötig für explorer/researcher)

## Non-Goals

- Rollout in alle Agents in diesem Bead (das ist Scope der folgenden Refactor-Beads #2 und #3)
- JSON-Format oder strukturierte Metadata-Felder (explizit verworfen in Design-Session)

## Related

Teil der "Single Source of Truth pro Session"-Architektur, die Hook-basierte Session-Summaries in open-brain ersetzt. Siehe open-brain-* Beads zu Stop-Hook-Removal und compact_memories.
notes:
[WAVE-REVIEW 2026-04-21]

Severity: HIGH (2), MEDIUM (1). Source: /wave-reviewer over Debrief-Chain.

### HIGH-1: Heading-Taxonomie-Kollision mit bestehendem session-close Step 11
session-close.md:199-203 sammelt heute bereits Debriefs mit den Sections:
Key Decisions / Challenges Encountered / Surprising Findings / Follow-up Items.
Dieser Bead schlug Discoveries / Decisions / Surprises / Follow-ups vor.

Entscheidung (User-Freigabe 2026-04-21): Contract adoptiert die existierende
Step-11-Taxonomie. Kein Fleet-Rename, Step 11 bleibt kompatibel.

Concrete AC-Update:
- Debrief-Template nutzt: Key Decisions / Challenges Encountered /
  Surprising Findings / Follow-up Items
- Agent-Update-Guide referenziert diese vier Headings verbindlich
- Standard-Doc (malte/standards/agents/debrief-contract.md) und Template-Block
  verwenden exakt diese Labels

### HIGH-2: Parser-Shape als aufrufbarer Python-Helper spezifizieren
Original-AC sagte "Parser-Reference in Python (<= 30 Zeilen) parse_debrief(text)".
bead-orchestrator ist aber Markdown LLM-Prompt -> kann kein Python importieren.

Entscheidung (User-Freigabe 2026-04-21): Helper wird als aufrufbares Script
implementiert, Consumer rufen via Bash auf.

Concrete AC-Update:
- Helper lebt unter beads-workflow/lib/orchestrator/parse_debrief.py
- Invocation-Contract: stdin = Subagent-Message, stdout = JSON mit vier Listen
  {key_decisions, challenges_encountered, surprising_findings, follow_up_items}
- Exit-Code 0 bei erfolgreichem Parse, !=0 bei Format-Verletzung
- Unit-Test (pytest) liegt neben dem Script, Sample-Input/Output im Standard
- CCP-5xe und CCP-4gi rufen diesen Helper auf, reimplementieren nicht

### MEDIUM-1: Kein Enforcer-Reactive fuer Contract-Adherence
Trinity-Pass: Bead liefert ADR (Standard) + Helper (Parser), aber kein
Reactive-Enforcer. Wenn Agent-Autor Debrief-Block vergisst, faellt nichts
loud. CCP-5xe sagt zusaetzlich "Subagents ohne Debrief = by design" (Explorer,
Researcher) -> forgot-vs-intentional ist nicht unterscheidbar.

Empfehlung: Follow-up Bead (nicht dieser) fuer Lint, der alle im
Agent-Update-Guide gelisteten Agents gegen Debrief-Template-Praesenz prueft.
NICHT blocking fuer diese Chain, aber als Issue tracken.

[WAVE-REVIEW 2026-04-21 — Option B approved]

### Neue AC (in Addition zum Original-Spec)

**AC-extension: Enforcer-Reactive Lint**
- Script: beads-workflow/scripts/check-debrief-adherence.py
- Input: Agent-Update-Guide-Liste aus dem Standard (malte/standards/agents/debrief-contract.md)
- Logik: Iteriert ueber Guide-Liste, fuer jeden gelisteten Agent pruefe
  dass dessen .md-Prompt den Debrief-Template-Block enthaelt (Heading
  "### Debrief" plus die vier h4 Headings "Key Decisions",
  "Challenges Encountered", "Surprising Findings", "Follow-up Items").
- Exit 0 wenn alle gelisteten Agents konform; Exit != 0 mit Report welcher
  Agent welche Section fehlt.
- Allowlist: Explorer, Researcher, general-purpose sind "by design" ohne
  Debrief -- werden NICHT geprueft. Allowlist liegt im selben Standard-Doc
  und wird vom Script eingelesen.
- Unit-Test (pytest): Mock-Agent-Verzeichnis mit (a) konformen Prompts,
  (b) fehlende Section, (c) allowlisted Agent ohne Section. Script
  liefert korrekte Pass/Fail-Verdikte.
- Non-Goal: Runtime-Check ob Agents tatsaechlich Debriefs emittieren.
  Lint prueft nur strukturelle Template-Praesenz. Runtime-Kontrolle
  waere Follow-up-Bead.

### Non-Goal-Klarstellung

Original sagte: "Rollout in alle Agents in diesem Bead (das ist Scope der
folgenden Refactor-Beads #2 und #3)". Das stimmt nicht ganz -- CCP-5xe
und CCP-4gi adressieren Orchestrator und session-close, nicht die
Subagent-Prompts selbst. Rollout ist jetzt ein eigener Bead (siehe
CCP-new, blocked on CCP-9bm).

title: CCP-c2p-followup: capture project-context --dry-run end-to-end transcript as AC#2 evidence
description:
## Context

Parent: CCP-c2p (Codex pilot, 3 skills, closed 2026-04-20 under SCOPE-EXPAND decision from iter-3 review).

CCP-c2p landed with honest-scope evidence (Option B): all 3 pilot skills validated via explicit-invocation + description round-trip, but no transcript captured a full workflow execution. The impl attempt at a project-context '--dry-run' transcript timed out and was deferred.

Iter-3 neutral review (Codex) disputed that 'description round-trip' satisfies the bead's literal AC#2 wording ('Codex discovers AND successfully invokes each of the 3 pilot skills end-to-end'). User decision was SCOPE EXPAND: merge CCP-c2p as-is, but capture the real end-to-end transcript as a follow-up and gate further rollout on it.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Run project-context with '--dry-run' on this repo via a fresh Codex session (from repo root, skill loaded from ~/.codex/skills/project-context/).
2. Capture the full transcript: Codex reading SKILL.md + Phase 1 gather (source files listed) + Phase 2 analysis (principles/invariants/module-map derived) + Phase 3 generated content printed inline (NOT written to disk) + '[DRY RUN - file not written]' footer.
3. Append the transcript to docs/codex-pilot-evidence.md as 'Invocation 1b: project-context end-to-end (dry-run)'.
4. Update the Scope of Evidence preamble to reflect that one of the three skills now has captured end-to-end evidence; spec-developer and bug-triage remain description-round-trip due to their interactive multi-round nature.
5. Update the closing Summary line to state: 'Phase 1 AC#2 satisfied: 1 of 3 skills end-to-end (project-context), 2 of 3 description-round-trip (spec-developer, bug-triage - multi-round interactive workflows).'

## Blocks

This bead blocks CCP-50y (10-skill rollout). Do NOT broaden the pilot to 10 skills until this evidence gap is closed or explicitly waived.

## Notes

- Follow-up from iter-3 review of CCP-c2p (2026-04-20). User chose SCOPE EXPAND over REVERT/ACCEPT.
- If '--dry-run' still times out on retry, the impl can instead capture a shorter exercise (e.g. Phase 1 gather only, with explicit '[scope truncated for pilot]' marker) - the point is ANY captured workflow execution, not a complete run.
close_reason: Satisfied by live Codex run of project-context on claude-code-plugins on 2026-04-21. User ran full (non-dry-run) project-context via ~/.codex/skills/project-context/ and it worked end-to-end — evidence stronger than the originally required --dry-run transcript (real file generation succeeded, not just content print). The live run also exposed the CLAUDE.md+AGENTS.md dual-source gap (CCP-5d0) which became the real discovery. Pilot is validated; CCP-50y unblocked.

title: Orchestrator token-capture: move response-to-file via Write tool, not shell heredoc
description:
## Context

Follow-up to CCP-2vo.1. Adversarial review iter 2 raised two BLOCKING concerns about
the Phase 4 token-capture template in `beads-workflow/agents/bead-orchestrator.md`
that were classified as design-robustness (not bead acceptance) and scope-expanded
here per user decision (option 3 in the CCP-2vo.1 iter-2 escalation).

Iter 1's original regression IS resolved in CCP-2vo.1 (commit 3922ad3) — the
`'''` / shell-metacharacter interpolation bug is gone, and `verification_tokens`
populates on normal runs. This bead addresses the edge-case hardening.

## Findings being deferred

### 1. [HIGH] Hard-coded heredoc delimiter `VERIFY_EOF` can collide with response content

Current Phase 4 template uses:

    cat > "$RESPONSE_FILE" << 'VERIFY_EOF'
    <PASTE_VERIFICATION_AGENT_RESPONSE_HERE>
    VERIFY_EOF

Quoted delimiter disables `$VAR` / backtick expansion (good). But if the
verification-agent response contains a line equal to `VERIFY_EOF`, the heredoc
closes early, the rest of the response is interpreted as shell input, and capture
fails silently (or worse, executes stray commands).

### 2. [MEDIUM, TEST_FALSE_PASS] New parse_usage tests don't exercise the capture path

`tests/test_verification_provenance.py::test_parse_usage_handles_triple_quotes_in_response`
and `test_parse_usage_handles_shell_metacharacters_in_response` call `parse_usage`
directly — but `parse_usage` was never the broken layer. Those tests would pass on
the pre-fix code. They validate parse_usage (useful) but NOT the capture recipe
that was actually buggy.

## Design fix (recommended)

Change the Phase 4 template so the orchestrator:
1. Writes the verification-agent response to a file via its native `Write` tool
   (not shell heredoc).
2. Passes the file path — not the response body — to the Python capture command.

This eliminates the entire "paste untrusted data into a shell command" class of
issue. Each iter 1→iter 2 finding was a different shell-quirk in that pattern;
removing the pattern removes all of them.

### Alternative (if native Write is not available to the orchestrator context)

Generate a unique heredoc delimiter per invocation (e.g. `VERIFY_$(uuidgen)_EOF`)
AND verify the delimiter does not appear in the payload before writing; retry
with a fresh delimiter if it does.

## Acceptance

- Phase 4 Token capture template in `bead-orchestrator.md` no longer embeds the
  response body in any shell command (heredoc, inline string, or otherwise).
- End-to-end test that exercises the actual capture recipe (not just `parse_usage`)
  with payloads containing `'''`, shell metacharacters, AND a line matching any
  heredoc delimiter the template might use.
- Existing 19 tests in `tests/test_verification_provenance.py` still pass.
- No new CHECK logic added to verification-agent (defer to CCP-2vo.9).

## Relation to siblings

- Parent: CCP-2vo (wave-orchestrator epic)
- Sibling: CCP-2vo.4 (orchestrator single-pane rewrite) may absorb this if its
  scope covers the Phase 4 template — decide at claim time.
- Dependency: CCP-2vo.1 (this is the follow-up to CCP-2vo.1's iter-2 findings).

## Background

Adversarial review thread for CCP-2vo.1 iter 2:
  019dab50-0aee-7ac1-8bb5-71de34835386
notes:
Provenance logged: standards=[workflow/tdd-discipline,workflow/test-quality,agents/tool-boundaries,python/style] skills=[] adrs=[none] docs=[none]
Break analysis: Phase 4 'Completion Verification' removed by CCP-2vo.4 rewrite. Provenance now in Phase 9; no active token capture recipe. 5 tests fail. Scope: add Phase 9 Token Capture using Write-tool pattern; update tests to reflect new structure; add adversarial E2E capture tests.
Phase 5 complete: 2 files changed (+199/-24), commits 1d2f3db (RED) + 849beb6 (GREEN). All 46 tests pass (was 38 passing / 5 failing). Phase 9 now has Token Capture subsection using Write-tool + file-path pattern — no heredoc, no embedded body. User stopped run at Phase 5; bead remains in_progress (review/verification/merge skipped).

title: Codex-Port wave/bead-orchestrator
description: Why: after the session-close agent port succeeds and CCP-2vo (single-pane wave mode — bead-orchestrator redesign) stabilizes in Claude, the orchestrator is the next Claude agent surface to bring to Codex. The orchestrator is the main autonomous entry point (claim, implement, review, verify, hand off) and has the widest blast radius; it is intentionally NOT the first port. What: port the single-pane bead-orchestrator (post-CCP-2vo shape) plus the wave-orchestrator to a Codex-usable form. Must reuse the session-close Codex port (CCP-9yd), not re-implement session-end.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A Codex-invokable bead-orchestrator exists at the Codex-discovery path confirmed by CCP-c2p Phase 0, reproducing the single-pane flow delivered by CCP-2vo (no cmux-reviewer pane, inline Codex review, axis-B auto-accept with veto gate).
2. A Codex-invokable wave-orchestrator exists and can dispatch multiple beads in dependency-aware waves using the Codex bead-orchestrator as its worker.
3. The orchestrator hands off to the session-close Codex agent (CCP-9yd) at the end of each bead — no duplicated session-end logic.
4. Per-call token usage is captured into metrics.db consistently with the Claude-side orchestrator (feature from CCP-2vo carries over).
5. Tool / capability gaps between Claude subagent orchestration and Codex agent orchestration are documented (spawn semantics, max_threads/max_depth, sandbox modes, MCP integration differences).
6. One end-to-end wave dispatch through Codex is evidenced on a non-critical bead set.
7. The orchestrator conforms to the portability-rules note produced by CCP-tkd and passes the CCP-u01 --check mode if derived via sync.
notes: Filed 2026-04-20 as the second Codex agent-port. Depends on CCP-9yd (session-close must land first so the orchestrator has something to hand off to) AND on CCP-2vo (the Claude-side single-pane redesign is the shape we port). Reference: docs/codex-skills-rollout-plan.md 'Nach dem Skill-Pilot'.

title: Codex-Port session-close agent
description: Why: session-close is the first Claude agent to be ported to Codex. It is the critical session-end path (double-merge main→feature, conventional commit + changelog, CalVer tag, learnings + session summary, feature→main merge + push, bd dolt commit/push, bead close) and has a relatively bounded surface — good first candidate after the 3-skill pilot succeeds. What: port the core:session-close Claude subagent to a Codex-usable form. Codex agent format: custom TOML agents in ~/.codex/agents/ plus built-ins default/worker/explorer. The port must map Claude subagent semantics (tool-whitelist, isolation, model override, frontmatter) onto Codex equivalents and explicitly document gaps.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A Codex-invokable session-close agent exists and is installed at the Codex-discovery path confirmed by CCP-c2p Phase 0 (likely ~/.codex/agents/).
2. The agent reproduces the double-merge strategy, changelog generation, CalVer tag, learnings extraction (open-brain), session summary, merge feature→main, git push, bd dolt commit/push, and bead-close sequence from the Claude version.
3. Tool / capability gaps between Claude subagents and Codex agents are documented in the agent file (e.g. what Codex can do natively vs what requires shell-out).
4. One end-to-end session-close run through Codex is evidenced (transcript or log) on a non-critical branch.
5. The agent conforms to the portability-rules note produced by CCP-tkd (portable core vs harness adapter).
6. Drift with the Claude-side counterpart is tracked: either via the CCP-u01 --check mode (if the Codex version is derived via sync) or by a note in the agent pointing at the source of truth.
notes:
Filed 2026-04-20 as the first Codex agent-port after skill pilot (CCP-50y). Depends on CCP-50y to ensure the skill-surface contract (portability rules, sync/check infrastructure) is in place before agents are ported. Reference: docs/codex-skills-rollout-plan.md 'Nach dem Skill-Pilot'.
Provenance logged: standards=[git/conventional-commits.md] skills=[/codex-guide] adrs=[none] docs=[docs/codex-skills-rollout-plan.md, docs/codex-skills-portability-rules.md, dev-tools/agents/codex-guide.md]
Break analysis: Codex agents path ~/.codex/agents/ does not exist yet (must create); session-close agent is complex (230+ lines Claude instructions); MCP open-brain IS configured in Codex config.toml (open-brain server) so memory tools work; bd commands work in both; hardest AK = AK4 (E2E Codex run evidence). Key gap: Claude Agent(subagent_type=...) has no direct Codex equivalent. Portability rules apply to developer_instructions content. No blockers.
Phase 3 arch review: council unavailable, fallback skipped — scope bounded to 1-2 new TOML files following established skill-porting pattern (CCP-c2p, CCP-50y). No new cross-cutting abstractions. Key decisions: (1) use ~/.codex/agents/ for personal scope, (2) developer_instructions = portable workflow, (3) gaps documented inline in TOML. CLEAN.
Review: status=FINDINGS-FIXED, quality=C, tdd=B, iter=1
Verification: VERIFIED. 25/25 bead tests pass. All 6 AKs independently confirmed. 6 pre-existing failures in unrelated tests (CCP-2vo.9, CCP-50y). Skill advisory: /codex-guide usage consistent with diff.
Close reason: Codex session-close agent ported; 25/25 tests pass; VERIFIED; review iter 1 FINDINGS-FIXED quality C tdd B; constraint PASS

title: [REFACTOR] replace Phase 3.6 token capture with ccusage + @ccusage/codex
description:
## Why

Today Phase 3.6 in bead-orchestrator.md contains ~60 lines of inline Python-in-Markdown that:
1. Asks the LLM to parse <usage> blocks from subagent responses
2. Builds a Python invocation string by hand
3. Executes it via Bash to write a row to ~/.claude/metrics.db

This is brittle (LLM can miscount, forget tokens, mis-attribute models), token-costly (60 lines re-read per bead), and captures a SMALL subset of what's actually available:
- No cache tokens (verified: 90-96% of total token count, paid at reduced rate)
- No USD cost
- No reasoning tokens (o1/o3/gpt-5-style)
- Only 21 rows since April 15 despite thousands of sessions

The data already exists on disk. Two published npm packages parse it correctly:
- ccusage — Claude Code JSONL in ~/.claude/projects/*/*.jsonl (4710 sessions locally)
- @ccusage/codex — Codex CLI JSONL in ~/.codex/

Directive: use these as runtime dependencies via bunx. No custom JSONL parsing.

## Verified facts (tested 2026-04-20)

- Aliases already present in ~/.zshrc:
  alias ccusage='bunx ccusage'
  alias ccusage-codex='bunx @ccusage/codex'
- Correct Codex subcommand is 'session' (singular), NOT 'sessions'. Daily / monthly / session are the three report types.
- ccusage session --json output shape (per entry in .sessions[]):
    sessionId, inputTokens, outputTokens, cacheCreationTokens, cacheReadTokens,
    totalTokens, totalCost (USD), lastActivity, modelsUsed, modelBreakdowns, projectPath
- sessionId encodes the cwd with '/' replaced by '-'. Example: a session from
    /Users/malte/code/claude-code-plugins/.claude/worktrees/bead-CCP-5ze
  appears as:
    -Users-malte-code-claude-code-plugins--claude-worktrees-bead-CCP-5ze
- Bead-id extraction regex (validated on historical data):
    jq 'capture("worktrees-bead-(?<b>.+)$").b'
  Works for all bead worktrees including mira-adapters and CCP prefixes.
- @ccusage/codex session --json output shape:
    sessionId, lastActivity, sessionFile, directory ('YYYY/MM/DD' only),
    inputTokens, cachedInputTokens, outputTokens, reasoningOutputTokens,
    totalTokens, costUSD, models.<model>.{...}
- Codex has NO cwd attribution. Bead-id correlation MUST be timestamp-window based.

## What

### 1. Handler: metrics-ingest.sh (shell, calls 'ccusage session --json')

Location: beads-workflow/lib/metrics/metrics-ingest.sh

Pipeline (pure shell + jq + sqlite3):
  SINCE=$1   # ISO date, e.g. 20260420
  bunx ccusage session --json --since "$SINCE" \
    | jq -c '.sessions[] | select(.sessionId | test("worktrees-bead-")) |
             {bead: (.sessionId | capture("worktrees-bead-(?<b>.+)$").b),
              total: .totalTokens, cost: .totalCost,
              cache_read: .cacheReadTokens, cache_create: .cacheCreationTokens,
              input: .inputTokens, output: .outputTokens}' \
    | while read row; do
        # UPSERT into metrics.db
      done

### 2. Handler: metrics-ingest-codex.sh

Location: beads-workflow/lib/metrics/metrics-ingest-codex.sh

Invoke:
  bunx ccusage-codex session --json --since "$SINCE"

Parse .sessions[]. Attribution by timestamp window:
- For each open/in_progress bead, read its claim-time from 'bd show --json'
- For each Codex session in the window, attribute to whichever bead was in_progress
- Accept approximate attribution for overlapping beads as a known limitation

### 3. metrics.db schema extension

ALTER TABLE bead_runs ADD COLUMN cache_read_tokens INTEGER DEFAULT 0;
ALTER TABLE bead_runs ADD COLUMN cache_creation_tokens INTEGER DEFAULT 0;
ALTER TABLE bead_runs ADD COLUMN reasoning_tokens INTEGER DEFAULT 0;
ALTER TABLE bead_runs ADD COLUMN cost_usd REAL DEFAULT 0.0;

Existing rows get zero defaults.

### 4. Delete Phase 3.6 from bead-orchestrator.md

Replace ~60 lines of inline Python/Bash with a single pointer:

  Token + cost capture is handled post-bead-close by session-close Step 16b
  via beads-workflow/lib/metrics/metrics-ingest.sh (Claude Code) and
  metrics-ingest-codex.sh (Codex). No action required here.

### 5. Session-close integration

Step 16b already iterates in-progress beads. After bd close, run both handlers
non-blocking:

  bash beads-workflow/lib/metrics/metrics-ingest.sh "$SINCE"
  bash beads-workflow/lib/metrics/metrics-ingest-codex.sh "$SINCE"

Failure of either logs a warning in Step 17 summary but does not abort session-close.

### 6. Optional: historic backfill (--backfill, one-off)

Re-ingest ALL historic JSONL to populate metrics.db for every bead we can attribute.
Script stays available but is not part of normal session-close flow. Idempotent
(UPSERT pattern, deduplicate by bead_id + date).

## Acceptance Criteria

- No package.json / node_modules in the repo — bunx handles everything
- metrics-ingest.sh uses 'bunx ccusage session --json', pipes through jq and sqlite3
- metrics-ingest-codex.sh uses 'bunx ccusage-codex session --json' (singular), parses via jq
- Both handlers UPSERT rows in ~/.claude/metrics.db
- metrics.db schema extended with cache_read_tokens, cache_creation_tokens, reasoning_tokens, cost_usd; existing rows default to zero
- bead-orchestrator.md Phase 3.6 reduced from ~60 lines to < 10 lines pointer
- session-close.md Step 16b calls both handlers non-blocking (warnings, not aborts)
- --backfill flag is idempotent
- shellcheck clean for both .sh handlers

## Scope boundaries

- Runtime dependency on ccusage and @ccusage/codex via bunx is INTENDED. No custom JSONL parsers.
- Codex cwd attribution uses timestamp-window correlation. Approximate is acceptable.
- bead_runs remains the business table (quality_grade, tdd_grade, wave_id, ak_count, phase2_*). ccusage only provides raw tokens/costs; it does NOT replace grading fields.
- Bun is a project prerequisite (already installed on dev machines).
- Do NOT delete the Python metrics.py lib — keep as the writer for orchestrator-authored rows.

## Dependency

Independent of CCP-ds0 and CCP-swh. Can start anytime.
acceptance_criteria: ccusage + @ccusage/codex runtime deps; metrics-ingest.mjs uses ccusage Node API; metrics-ingest-codex.sh invokes @ccusage/codex --json; metrics.db schema extended with cache + cost + reasoning columns; Phase 3.6 in bead-orchestrator reduced to <10 lines; session-close Step 16b calls both handlers non-blocking; --backfill is idempotent; no custom JSONL parsing anywhere
close_reason: ingest_ccusage.py + ingest_codex.py; 91 beads backfilled with $328.72 cost history

title: [REFACTOR] session-close: trim LLM prose, script-render summary, dedupe tail
description:
## Why

After CCP-ds0 extracted handlers from session-close.md, the file is still 634 lines (~9500 tokens re-sent on every invocation). A token-cost audit found that ~115 lines (~1500 tokens) are not genuine LLM work but rather:

- Design documentation / tutorial prose the LLM does not need at runtime
- Redundant cross-references (same concern stated 3-4 times across Error Handling / Do NOT / step prose / Flags Reference)
- Pure string templates the LLM today formats by hand

## What (three coordinated sub-changes)

### 1. Move tutorial prose to handler headers

- Step 16a: delete the three explainer blocks — Why / Registration vs completion / Registration-fail distinction (~15 lines). Their content moves to the header comment of pipeline-watch.sh where humans reading the script will find it.
- Step 15b: delete the SemVer vs CalVer explanation prose (~8 lines). Handler version.sh already detects the strategy; the explanation belongs in version.sh header.
- Step 16b: delete the 'Prerequisite' + 'CRITICAL' block at the top (~6 lines) — the Step 16a decision tree already states 'do not run 16b on failed'; repeating it at Step 16b is redundant.

Expected savings: ~30 lines / ~400 tokens.

### 2. Static tail dedupe — each concern said once

- Error Handling table becomes THE canonical reference. Per-step 'On conflict: STOP, report' prose shrinks to 'Handler exit 2 — see Error Handling'.
- Do NOT list trimmed to cross-cutting rules only (never git push --force, never git add -A, never git rebase). Per-step specific 'Do NOT run Step 16b when ...' items removed — the step prose already covers it.
- Flags Reference remains the only flag semantics source. Any step mentioning a flag links to it rather than re-explaining.
- Multi-Repo Awareness (8 lines): already mostly implied by per-repo handling in Steps 6/16 — collapse to a 2-line note or delete.

Expected savings: ~40 lines / ~500 tokens.

### 3. render-summary.py handler for Step 17 Technical Summary

Currently Step 17 has a bullet-list template the LLM fills by hand from state it has been tracking across 15 steps. This is error-prone (missed fields) and token-costly (the template itself is ~15 lines of prose).

New handler: render-summary.py takes JSON state on stdin or via --state arg, emits the formatted Technical Summary block. The LLM becomes a state-assembler; rendering is deterministic.

State schema (example):
{
  'commit_sha': '...', 'commit_msg': '...', 'version_tag': 'v2026.04.47',
  'changelog_updated': true, 'doc_gaps': [...],
  'learnings_extracted': true, 'session_summary_saved': true,
  'turn_log_status': 'uploaded_deleted',
  'merge_from_main_first': 'success', 'merge_from_main_second': 'success',
  'worktree_merged': true, 'push_status': 'success',
  'pipeline_status': 'passed', 'pipeline_run_url': 'https://...'
}

The 'What's New' section STAYS as LLM prose in Step 17 — it needs semantic translation (bead title -> user-facing capability) that scripts cannot do. Only the Technical Summary below it becomes script-rendered.

Expected savings: ~15 lines / ~200 tokens + correctness (no more 'forgot to include pipeline status' bugs).

### Total expected impact

- session-close.md: 634 -> ~520 lines (~18% shrinkage)
- Tokens per invocation: ~1100-1500 less re-read
- Correctness: deterministic summary rendering, no redundant cross-references drifting out of sync

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] pipeline-watch.sh and version.sh header comments contain the tutorial content that was removed from session-close.md (Why / Registration vs completion / SemVer vs CalVer)
- [ ] Step 16a has no 'Why' / 'Registration vs completion' / 'Registration-fail distinction' prose blocks
- [ ] Step 15b has no SemVer-vs-CalVer explanation prose
- [ ] Step 16b has no Prerequisite/CRITICAL duplicate block at the top
- [ ] Error Handling table is referenced from per-step prose instead of repeated; per-step 'STOP if conflict' lines shortened to a handler-exit pointer
- [ ] Do NOT list contains only cross-cutting rules (never force-push, never add -A, never rebase) — no per-step duplicates
- [ ] Flags Reference unchanged; no flag semantics appear in step prose beyond a cross-reference
- [ ] Multi-Repo Awareness section collapsed to <=3 lines or deleted
- [ ] render-summary.py exists in session-close-handlers/, stdlib only, accepts JSON state via --state <file> or stdin, emits the Technical Summary block to stdout
- [ ] Step 17 Technical Summary subsection replaced by a single handler invocation — the 'What's New' subsection is unchanged (still LLM prose)
- [ ] session-close.md line count drops from 634 to ~520 (±20)
- [ ] No behavioural change — Step 16a gating, double-merge strategy, dry-run still work identically
- [ ] shellcheck clean for any edited .sh handlers; python3 ast.parse OK for render-summary.py

## Scope boundaries

- Refactor only. No new features, no behaviour changes.
- 'What's New' stays LLM prose (genuine semantic work).
- Step 16b close-reason composition stays LLM prose (judgment).
- Step 6 interactive commit stays LLM prose.
- Do NOT touch other handlers (pipeline-watch, merge-from-main, merge-feature, turn-log-upload) beyond adding header comments.

## Dependency

Follows CCP-ds0 (which extracted turn-log / merge handlers). Can start immediately after that closes.
acceptance_criteria: Tutorial prose moved to handler headers; static tail deduped (Error Handling as single source); render-summary.py renders Step 17 Technical Summary from JSON state; What's New stays LLM prose; session-close.md shrinks to ~520 lines; no behavioural change; shellcheck + python3 ast.parse clean
notes:
Grading: quality=A tdd=N/A review_iterations=0 (GSD mode, [REFACTOR] title)
Close reason: session-close.md 653→533 lines (18.4% reduction); render-summary.py added (stdlib only, stdin/--state); tutorial prose moved to pipeline-watch.sh + version.sh headers; Do NOT trimmed to 4 cross-cutting rules; shellcheck clean + python3 ast.parse OK
close_reason: Refactor merged in 93869f6 (trimmed session-close.md 653→533 lines, added render-summary.py, moved tutorial prose to handler headers). Co-Author line issue resolved by other session's overwrite.

title: [REFACTOR] session-close: extract handlers + dedupe docs
description:
## Why

session-close.md is at 700 lines and bloats on each session-close. Three extractions would cut ~130 lines without changing behaviour:

1. Step 12c contains a 70-line Python heredoc inline in bash — worst-case code location.
2. Steps 1/14 (first/second merge from main) and Step 15 (feature to main) repeat similar git invocations inline.
3. Flags are listed in Input section AND enumerated in Flags Reference table — redundant.

## What

1. Extract Step 12c Python to `session-close-handlers/turn-log-upload.py` (stdlib only). Step 12c shrinks to env-var setup + handler call.
2. Consolidate Steps 1 and 14 into `merge-from-main.sh`. Extract Step 15 into `merge-feature.sh`.
3. Remove the Input-section flags list (keep Flags Reference table at the end) and the duplicate 'Step order within Phase B' note.

## Acceptance Criteria

- turn-log-upload.py exists in session-close-handlers/, stdlib only, env-var interface unchanged
- Step 12c in session-close.md shrinks to setup + handler call (< 30 lines)
- merge-from-main.sh handles fetch+merge origin/main, called from Steps 1 and 14
- merge-feature.sh --main-repo <dir> --branch <b> handles Step 15
- Steps 1, 14, 15 call handlers (< 10 lines each)
- Input-section redundant flag list removed; Step order duplicate note removed
- shellcheck clean for new .sh handlers; python3 -c compiles the .py handler
- session-close.md line count drops from 700 to ~570 (±20)
- No behavioural change — Step 16a gating, double-merge, dry-run all still work

## Scope

- Refactor only.
- Keep env-var interface for turn-log-upload.py.
- Do NOT extract Step 13 kill-worktree or Step 16 push — out of scope.

title: [TASK] Enhance bead creation with trinity-aware checklist and cross-bead readiness prompts
description:
## Context

`beads-workflow/skills/create/SKILL.md` already handles type classification, feature scenarios, and an opt-in `touches-contract` template. But recent wave-level review showed that higher-quality beads need more than local bead prose: they need explicit ownership, lifecycle side effects, validation shape, dependency rationale, and branch-case acceptance criteria. The current create flow does not systematically elicit these.

There is also an Architecture Trinity model in the repo (ADR / Helper / Enforcer-Proactive / Enforcer-Reactive) that should inform bead writing whenever a bead touches contracts or creates enforcement work.

## Scope

Enhance the bead generation process so newly created beads are less likely to fail later wave review.

Potential directions to evaluate and implement:
- A bead-writing checklist in `create` for ownership, lifecycle impact, dependency rationale, negative-path coverage, and validation method
- Stronger prompts when `touches-contract` is selected, using Trinity terminology consistently
- Optional prompts for whether the bead is part of migration / validation / cleanup / steady-state work
- Guidance for when a bead should trigger wave-level review because risks are cross-bead rather than local

## Acceptance Criteria

- Proposal grounded in current `create` skill and related bead-planning docs
- Resulting bead-generation checklist includes at least: contract touched, owner of interface/schema/helper, lifecycle side effects, dependency rationale, acceptance branch cases, and non-goals
- Trinity terms are used correctly where relevant
- Follow-up implementation stays compatible with current `bd create` workflow

## Notes

This bead is for improving bead quality at creation time. It complements `/wave-reviewer`, which catches system-level defects after multiple beads exist.

title: Delete cmux-reviewer + cld -br + companion AFTER A/B validation passes
description:
## Context

## Scope — delete AFTER validation

Prerequisite: CCP-2vo.8 validation complete, verdict "keep new flow."

2. **Remove `cld -br` launcher support** (search `cld` script / alias / wrapper for `-br` / `--bead-review` flag, delete dispatch logic)
3. **Delete `codex-companion.mjs` and its state directory** (`~/.claude/plugins/data/codex-openai-codex/state/`) IF no remaining callers exist. Audit first.
4. **Delete `codex-watch.sh`** if flagged dead in CCP-2vo.6 audit. Otherwise leave and document retention reason.
5. **Update CHANGELOG** noting the topology removal.
